speaker: interviewer
text: --1995. The survivor being interviewed-- Doctor Esther Fox. Interviewer is Miryam Rabner. We're in New York City, United States of America. This is tape number four. OK, so you continued to live like this in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: But he's ready? Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Every day, you just got through the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the conditions got worse. There was less food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Less food and less of everything. To me, it was less [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] willpower to-- to-- to stay, to go-- to go on. But all right, I didn't say--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they taking more and more people out of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. When the-- when it came to the-- the Russian approached, so they liquidated the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And people have to report to a certain point. And they took them. And-- and-- and-- and they was going around with cars, trucks, and collected the people who couldn't go anymore. And I remember I was sitting, and my mother and my brother, sitting and waiting. Because for a few days, we didn't have anything to eat. And I had enough. And my mother, we were depressed. So we said, let them come and take us. And what will be will be. Yeah. Now, My brother packed a-- a case with books. As an engineer-- we didn't know where we are going. He thought maybe he will be an engineer.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he took the books which might be useful for him, if he has to work. And we made up that I will not say that I am a doctor so that they shouldn't divide us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And with my mother, so this is the mother. So that's all. So-- and I took my diploma, which I put in, again, here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we waited they should, to take us. And we waited they should take us. Because how long can you wait? Two days? Because we lived in a-- [? this ?] what the street.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we changed also, at the end, at night. One night, we slept here. One night, somewhere else. We didn't know what to do. Until they came and they took us.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did they come and take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember the date. It was the day after my birthday, the 17 of August in '44. They-- we went to the-- to the-- to the trains, which took us. Which I found out it is Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of trains? Cattle trains or people trains?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The-- the-- the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Cattle trains?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's right. No seat, no-- no-- no air, a little window, maybe. And they closed those.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many were you in one wagon?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, like-- like sardines.

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't tell you. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many wagons were there on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't-- I couldn't tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were together still, with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my--

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you did not know where they're taking you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you ride in the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a half an hour?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. It was long.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or was it 10 hours?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was longer. Because--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you food?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not a drop of water?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. And I remember my mother developed diarrhea. And other people developed those, and vomiting, and this everything, together. It was no-- no-- no-- no--

speaker: interviewer
text: No facilities?

speaker: interviewee
text: No facilities whatsoever. So it was hell on earth.

speaker: interviewer
text: No air coming in?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you stand up or did you sit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stand up. There was nowhere to sit. But with-- it was so packed that one hold the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk to each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe. They didn't forbid to talk. There were no German inside. I don't think so, no. From outside, they closed us.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had no idea where they're taking you.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [BACKGROUND NOISE] Who did this, there?

speaker: interviewee
text: (WHISPERS) Oh my God.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened? All of a sudden, the train stopped?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they opened the door. And they started like we know from movies. I think everybody knows it, [GERMAN]. And over the head, with the hitting. Not everybody could go fast. And we were exhausted. And some were already dead. They took out the dead people too from-- from my train, from the whole train. And-- and we saw that the German and-- and-- and these who helped them were Jews or not. We didn't know who is who. And they took us on a march a little bit. And people were separated with wire, whatever. And they-- and we got a supply, a piece of bread and then something. They gave us--

speaker: interviewee
text: So they started counting from their side, throw us the bread. Throw us the sugar. They will take it away from you anyhow. So we didn't know to give it to them or not to give to them. So-- and I looked at them.

speaker: interviewee
text: They all were in rags. They all shaven heads and-- and-- and emaciated. So I-- I-- right away, I got confused.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm talking about myself. And I thought, is this an asylum for-- for mentally ill? Or-- or did something happen to me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I-- it looked like an asylum, which in rags, and then they don't know what they're talking-- with shaven heads, and the eyes like that. So I thought I am there, a mental-- something. And we-- I don't know where I'm going, be one of them right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: In which language did they scream to you to throw the bread?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish, Polish. They were all, you know, Jews, Polish. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Nobody told you they're killing us here or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they didn't say anything. We didn't know what's going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you walk to? You walked someplace when you got off the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: To what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you walk to?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- where they were leading us. I couldn't tell you where we went. We went--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, did you come to a room?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. First, there was a selection, who will live and who will go. So-- so-- they didn't accommodate us yet, to take us out. I don't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the selection? In the outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, somewhere. Out on the fields. I couldn't tell you. I don't know what it was. But yeah, so later came-- where-- later, I found out. That time, I didn't know it was Mengele.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- you-- somebody made a selection and later--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. We all came together. And they-- later were men separate somehow, and women were separate. And later came, where it came my turn.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I saw somebody standing. I didn't know that this was Mengele. But it was Mengele. And, you know, he looked. And we were naked. When did they take away your clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we had to march through him, somewhere. And whoever looked emaciated, or had a rash or something, back. And who would look not so bad, he could use them eventually. And one friend of mine, she was clean. She thought the cleaner will be better. She had white stockings.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he thought that it is a cast. So she-- she somehow pulled it down. The-- I mean, to such a degree, she survived. Now she's in Stockholm. And-- So my mother disappeared right away. I didn't know where, what. And my brother disappeared-- You never said goodbye to her.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- well, yeah, yeah. Now I remember. My brother, they-- the men took on the one side, before we are-- we started marching. Later, we started marching.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this I-- I didn't know what I'm going, the right-- is it? Or the bad side? I didn't know, altogether, what's going on. So from there, they took us. They called us the-- the sauna, the-- the-- the-- the bath. So I didn't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't think. Because I was confused. And when we were in this bath, one of whom I knew from my hometown, they-- she said, they're going to gas us. I never thought of it. I never wanted to think. And it didn't bother me. [? Let me ?] [? be ?] gassed. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother was gone.

speaker: interviewee
text: She wasn't already with me, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: You never saw her again?

speaker: interviewee
text: She went right away to the other side.

speaker: interviewer
text: You never saw mother again?

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- so-- but it wasn't gas. Because after we showered, whatever you call it, they let us go. And there were high piles of-- of-- of clothes. So-- and the German was with the-- with the thing, hit the--

speaker: interviewer
text: With a whip?

speaker: interviewee
text: With a whip, hit [GERMAN], fast. So I grabbed kind of a skirt which was a real wool. Later-- and this-- this was August, right? And I grabbed a little blouse which was small. So which I was here, open, not together. But I couldn't look for any-- and no shoes, no nothing. And they started marching someplace. So I grabbed [? the ?] [? long ?] [? skirt. ?] And all right. There was no trying to look or to-- and I couldn't walk without shoes. So do you know, one on the other. And people, you know, are excited. They start to look me, at this princess. She cannot walk. No, I cannot on the-- the-- the others may be [? the ?] [? poorer ?] [? where ?] [? you was ?] [? from. ?] I wasn't. And later, they took us to a barrack. And I think barrack 20, whatever, it was considered the worst, they said.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in this barrack, there were perches, yeah? It's under here and high and higher. So trying to remember. Yeah, the-- no. I mix it up, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was-- so we accommodate somewhere. I was on the floor. I couldn't climb up. They didn't let me there. I wasn't so-- so agile to do it. So on the floor. And over there, it was started being appell, in the morning. And yeah, before they put us, we were hours, a whole half a day and a night. And it was cold. And the girls-- not me-- started already, having, also, diarrhea. And there was no-- no-- no accommodations.

speaker: interviewee
text: So as we were, we're everything, together. This is undescribable. I am now getting confused already, how it was. Because everybody was dirty from-- from different way, up from down.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't remember how we went, came already, to the barrack. And over there were again, barrack. So we were standing in lines of five. So I always got in the wrong place. But I was always late. I-- I wasn't so-- to think so fast. So five of us in the first rows was the first to get over the head. And they gave a soup in a plate for the five of us. No spoon, no nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I remember I wanted-- after a day or two, I got, already, a little bit acquainted, what's going on, that will take two sips. That wouldn't come back to me. So they thought that I want to eat the whole soup. So and that had to go. And-- and-- and with the hand, the potato, I don't remember exactly how it was. Because I was mixed up.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so this was more or less like--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they shave you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I forgot. You see that. When-- [SIGHS] When we went out from the shower-- we took a shower, [? cold. ?] They shaved us. And you know, all over-- all over. And when we--

speaker: interviewer
text: All body hair?

speaker: interviewee
text: All body. And we walked out. We were a couple. Because our transport from our town came the same day, the same time. We knew each other. We didn't recognize each other. Because so we have to call by name to know who is who. Because we didn't recognize, anymore, who-- who we are.

speaker: interviewee
text: So with the shaven heads, a criminal. And-- and with the blouse here, opened. And with the skirt-- so somebody said, the winter will be good, will be warm. You know? So this is what it was. If-- I don't remember anymore. Now, maybe it comes like-- Did they tattoo you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh? No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: The first five days-- now it will come, what happened to me. Yeah, one day came over there a German. I forgot her name. She was with the-- with the dog and with the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Whip.

speaker: interviewee
text: --with the whip. And she asked who knows to sing? So there was a Greek girl. They didn't cut her hair. She had long hair. Maybe the-- only the-- the-- [NON-ENGLISH], the East Jews, they did it. The Hungarian, also, didn't have shaved, cut short. But only we.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she says, who knows to sing? So it was the Greek girl. And she stepped out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was singing 'Ave Maria.' And this German, head was leaning against the wall. And tears were running down from emotion. So I said, maybe some transformation will come. Because she was known as a devil with the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Whip.

speaker: interviewee
text: --with the whip and with the dog. So this 'Ave Maria,' and the tears, I thought, no. When the girl finished, she started beating her with the whip after this. Maybe because she-- she's so-- she sang so beautiful. Couldn't tell [? her age. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this a Jewish Greek girl or a Christian?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Jewish. So-- in this block were only Jewish. So to show you a German intelligence, they are musical. You know, they love music. She was emotional and very moved with 'Ave Maria.' But later, the-- the-- the hatred came. And she, almost-- and this were a kid, the girl. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So now, one day came somebody. Can't remember, military or not military man and said, are there here, some doctors? So we were four from our town. So one was with the sister. One was with the daughter and one was-- I don't remember, and I. So they took me because I was alone. I'm the [NON-ENGLISH], [? the ?] [? person. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they don't want two. They didn't have to ask. They could separate.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyhow, I was the youngest from them. How could they recognize with that hair old, young? And they took me out from the whole trouble.

speaker: interviewee
text: This why I don't have the number. But it didn't go so fast. So many transports came from all sides, every day. And they gave me a pair of shoes which didn't fit me. But at least shoes. And they gave me a dress, no underwear or nothing. And they told me that I'm going someplace. And they took a soldier with a rifle, everything, by train. And we traveled to Guben-- Guben.

speaker: interviewer
text: You, alone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: With that German soldier?

speaker: interviewee
text: With soldier. And I-- he had the papers that I am the doctor assigned to do-- to this Arbeitslager.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he-- the dress, was it civilian dress? Or was it a striped dress?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, a civilian. Because I was supposed to be a-- a-- a humanitarian help to give them. You understand?

speaker: interviewer
text: So you left Auschwitz after a few days?

speaker: interviewee
text: After a few days, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you travel in that train with the--

speaker: interviewee
text: A couple hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: In a regular passenger train?

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't touch me. He didn't beat me. He didn't talk to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a passenger train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So now we came over there. And there was a-- a woman, the officer in the supervising of this Arbeitslager. It wasn't an extermination camp, nothing. They worked in a factory and they needed a doctor, that-- so that they should be able to work and produce for the-- for the war machine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who worked in the factory? Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: These girl, only Jewish. And these girl were 99% Hungarian. And I was the only Pole. And I was the only one-- later, I found out-- with shaven head.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when she looked at me-- when I came, he gave her the papers. So she asked me in German, understand, what is my crime? She thought, I think so, the head. So I answered her again, in German, my crime is that I am Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because there was no other reason to call me crime. So at that moment, she wasn't too bad to say. But she could take out the-- the-- the whatever and give me right away, or send me back to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: How do I dare to open the mouth? And she was wearing a uniform with a swastika, with everything. But I told her that my crime is that I am Jewish. Guben? Guben is near Breslau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Near Breslau. And the-- I-- if I remember well, the girls were working in the factory of electrical wires, supplies. And this was a [? Lawrence ?] factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many girls were in that camp? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, about 100, more maybe. I couldn't tell you. So when I settled-- she-- so she-- everything fine for me, the doctor. In the [NON-ENGLISH], like, the hospital, was a room for me, and water, and hot water for the hospital and me. And I was-- I was surprised. And she tells me, now comes a crucial moment so in my life. She told me I should sit down and write orders, what she-- supply, what I need to--

speaker: interviewee
text: To run the hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: --to run the hospital. So I sat down. And I didn't remember a thing. I didn't remember my name, by the way. Because the shock from the few days in Auschwitz, what they-- I didn't remember. But-- so maybe she didn't insist. I didn't know. But now she left, and left me in the room with paper and a pencil, I should write the supply. I don't remember one word. So I said, because now she will think I'm not a doctor. Now she will think I am crazy. Because I remember the others over there, crazy. And I thought maybe I am crazy now.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I started thinking in pictures. When I was called to a patient, what-- how was it? So we had such a special bag, which doctors have here too, but another one. And I took the bag and I climbed stairs, whatever. And I examined a patient. And later I sat down and I wrote. What did I wrote? So the first word came to me, [NON-ENGLISH]. That time was in style, you know-- A painkiller?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, no. [NON-ENGLISH], from Switzerland. And from-- I had [NON-ENGLISH]. So I got aspirin. Uh-huh. So my memory probably started working.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I reminded myself. I wrote cotton, alcohol, iodines, a few-- a bandage. And she came. And she said, that's all? I couldn't tell her that I don't remember anything. So I said, for the moment, that's all. I don't remember, but then I was so smart to tell her, for the moment. Anyhow, she let me go. And later, gradually, I became back to earth. And I told that I need this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, she couldn't give me more. A bandage was from paper. So I rolled on paper. And the girls-- so now we will-- I am already settled.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the girls are going to-- to the factory. So five o'clock in the morning was the appell. And how do you call it? Appell.

speaker: interviewee
text: Appell. And I did-- had to be there. And I gave a list, already, when I working, that so many are in the-- in the hospital, in the [NON-ENGLISH]. So that when they counted that they knew, five, six, eight, whatever, are missing, are with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was standing there. And I was dressed, and they were all shivering. It was cold and hungry. You know, five o'clock, they hardly had a few hours' sleep. And all were wearing the-- the Holland shoes. The-- how do you call it? The-- the-- the-- The clogs.

speaker: interviewee
text: The clogs. And when they came back, they marched miles. I don't know. They all have cut the-- the-- the-- the-- the-- the-- the skin here. And I bandaged with this paper band-aid. Before I finished, it was already ripped. It wasn't good. And so the-- the-- the-- the wound was deeper and deeper. Because it never healed.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were most of these young women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Over they were mostly young. Because they needed-- they didn't do hard, physical work. So they selected them to do-- it was with wires.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's young? 20?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. There were a few, 14.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: 15. Why do I remember this? Because Hungarian, they were the youngest and kids. And they-- they-- I spoke German. They all speak-- from Budapest, they all speak German.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the-- the supervisor, the German who asked me what's my-- my crime, once came to me. She had pity on them, I think. And she said, you know, take these three girls.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were kids, 15 years, maybe 12, and make them sick. And let them work in the kitchen. And not in the hospital, but in the kitchen.

speaker: interviewee
text: So meanwhile, they will eat potatoes. You know, you-- you don't-- they didn't count the potatoes over there. And-- and let them recover a little bit. So in the first moment, I thought maybe she wants to trick me in. I said, so even if, let it be. We'll see. But she didn't really. They were [? a bit-- ?] but it didn't help them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one said my father was a doctor. I came to see, somewhere-- I came to see my grandmother and they caught me. So this what she wanted. But at the same time, she didn't beat anybody. But once, one girl, she-- I don't know what she did. She ask her to-- to-- to knee, on one knee and put a glass of-- with water on the head.

speaker: interviewee
text: And if she-- the-- the water shouldn't run off and spill out. So how long can you be, not to move? And I couldn't watch too much. Because first of all, I couldn't watch either.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and I have to do something. But this what she-- she was cruel. She didn't beat her, but it's worse than beaten.

speaker: interviewee
text: So now my work, I had a double bookkeeping, the temperature. I had one for the German. Because every once in a while came a German, a doctor or some military man, in the uniform. And he asked me to show that so for him, everybody had good temperature. They all had TB, you know? And other things too.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, one had-- everybody had the skin. And one had diarrhea. One had a cut here. But I didn't tell the truth. And I-- I was risking. But so I risk. So I had to do something.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they would kill them.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they would take them out right away, back to Auschwitz. And do that again. So, all right, so this was the thing. And so I had it over there. Yeah. After a while, they brought another transport. There were a few Polish from my hometown. So I felt better because the Hungarians don't like Polish either, and I couldn't talk. All right, German doesn't matter to them. So over there was one young girl. She was a-- a daughter of a doctor, my next-door neighbor. So I adopted her, like my child. And I told her, after work, come right away to me. And I made her she has to wash herself, not to neglect, because I had water. And I save for her, if I could, a potato or something. And a little warmer was in my-- in the-- in the hospital. And so she didn't survive, and that's all. But I said there came a few. And-- and they-- they-- they were hungry there too. Because it was a shortage of food for the German too. Maybe not for the real in Berlin, they had it that good. But in the other places. But she wasn't the worst. Because she could be worse. They-- they did the right thing. She could with me, they could give me a-- give me a bullet in the head. She didn't. And finally, came the time when the Russian approached.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you, you never slept in the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You always slept in the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had better conditions, again, because you were the physician.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a doctor. But here. Because later comes out it was different. It's not yet my end--

speaker: interviewer
text: I know.

speaker: interviewee
text: --of the career. So here I have-- I was the doctor, [? I ?] [? was ?] [? today. ?] And she trusted. She didn't check with me. And I-- I made the little-- so somebody wasn't so sick, I kept her a day, whole.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did anybody menstruate still? Or everything stopped?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody. This is called [INAUDIBLE] amenorrhea, from the shock, and I myself too, right? I was-- they said they poisoned-- they gave something in the food. I was only six or seven days in Auschwitz. And the seventh-- this is emotional. And this is called [INAUDIBLE] amenorrhea. Yeah. And I want to say that the girls told me that in the factory, there are some-- there were some people who gave them a piece of bread and gave them an apple sometimes. And once, one brought-- she got, from somebody, stockings. And they gave me a pair of stockings.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I didn't put them on. Because first of all, she'd be asking where from I have stockings? And I didn't have with what to hold them. So I didn't put them on. But I got, I remember, a-- a-- an act of spasm. Somebody gave me something. A girl, you understand? What, I am supposed to help her.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- so this was an emotional-- some-- some public human relation. So I get a pair of stockings. I didn't put them on because I was afraid of her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because if I wear, they say, who gave it to me? So they-- so the German gave her. So it-- it's better not, and that's all. So this, I just want to say. So now when it came the time that probably the Russian approached, they liquidated. They packed. We go. When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- we came over there in-- in February, sometimes in the beginning of February, end of January.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was winter. It was cold. And they-- they came different transport from others on the way to-- to join and go together.

speaker: interviewee
text: So came a doctor from another camp with a transport and said to me, make your people go. Because if you say you are sick, they shoot them out right away, what they do with her. So I have to tell the men that were sick, but how can I talk? There is a German, always. I can't talk Hungarian.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I cannot tell them in German, this message. But somehow, I managed to tell them. But what is-- Some said, shoot or not shoot, I can't go. Let me shoot, then. And I didn't volunteer. There was another. Yeah, I forgot to tell you, there came another doctor from Yugoslavia. She survived to live in Israel. And we are still good friends. And we didn't volunteer because we didn't have the right to say what we want. But the German came and put us on the truck. So all right. So we go, that we'll be shot, like with the others. Fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they tell you where they're going to take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. So-- so with-- so meanwhile, the others marched. And I talked in my-- adopted daughter, go. And others whom I could, go. Because this what she told me. And these who said they cannot, and I was on the-- on the truck too. And we traveled a long time, day, night. Because I know it was day. It was night, open. And it was snowing. And we were full of snow. And on the truck we saw that they are dead bodies. All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So finally, we came. I don't know what it is again, where we are. But this was Bergen-Belsen. You heard about it.
